food_subgroup: Prepared salmon dishes — grilled and aburi (flame-torched) salmon

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for grilled caramelised salmon or aburi salmon. FOODON_03302114 (salmon food product) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Salmon, cooked) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for aburi or caramelised preparation variants.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for grilled caramelised salmon or aburi salmon as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving consists of approximately 150 g of grilled caramelised and aburi (flame-torched) salmon fillet as served at a Japanese sushi restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Fish, salmon, Atlantic, farmed, cooked, dry heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base nutrient values derived from USDA SR Legacy entry for Atlantic salmon, farmed, cooked by dry heat (FDC ID 175168). Caramelisation glaze (soy sauce, mirin, sugar) adds approximately 3–5 g carbohydrate and 150–200 mg sodium per 100 g serving. Adjusted carbohydrate_g and sodium_mg upward to reflect typical teriyaki-style caramelised preparation. Protein and fat values retained from USDA base. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for grilled caramelised salmon or aburi salmon. GI estimated at 35 based on the analogous food category of teriyaki-glazed fish dishes. The dominant carbohydrate source is the caramelisation glaze (mirin, sugar, soy sauce). Mirin and sugar-based glazes have a moderate GI (~55–65 for pure sugar), but the very low carbohydrate content of the overall dish (4.5 g per 100 g) results in a low glycemic load. A conservative GI of 35 is assigned consistent with mixed protein-dominant dishes with small sugar-based glaze components, as supported by the University of Sydney GI Database framework for protein-dominant cooked fish dishes. GL per 150 g serving = (35 × 6.75 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 2.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and caramelisation glaze
hypertension.guidance: Each 150 g serving provides approximately 570 mg sodium, representing about 25% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-sodium soy sauce or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic load and high protein content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic load of approximately 2.4 per serving and 22 g protein per 100 g, this dish has minimal impact on postprandial blood glucose and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acid content from Atlantic salmon
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Atlantic salmon is a rich source of EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids (~1.5–2 g per 100 g), which are associated with reduced triglycerides and cardiovascular risk; however, the sodium content from the glaze warrants attention in patients with concurrent hypertension.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled Caramelised Salmon and Aburi Salmon is a Japanese-style salmon dish served at Sushi Express and similar Japanese casual dining restaurants in Singapore. The dish combines two preparation styles: grilled caramelised salmon, where the fillet is cooked over direct heat and glazed with a sweet soy or teriyaki-style sauce that caramelises on the surface to create a slightly sticky, sweet-savoury crust; and aburi salmon, where the salmon is flame-torched (aburi technique) to partially cook and char the surface while leaving the interior tender and silky. The result is a contrast of textures — a lightly charred, caramelised exterior with a moist, fatty interior. Atlantic salmon is the typical species used, prized for its high fat content, rich flavour, and vibrant orange flesh. Nutritionally, this dish is protein-dense (approximately 22 g protein per 100 g) and rich in omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids including EPA and DHA, which support cardiovascular health. The caramelisation glaze contributes a modest amount of carbohydrate (approximately 4.5 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium (~380 mg per 100 g). The glycemic load per serving is low (~2.4), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is dairy-free and gluten-containing due to soy sauce. It is commonly served as part of a bento set or as a standalone main at Japanese sushi restaurants in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy data for Atlantic salmon cooked by dry heat (FDC ID 175168), with upward adjustments to carbohydrate and sodium to account for the caramelisation glaze. No exact HPB Singapore or USDA Branded Food entry exists for this specific restaurant dish. GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database framework for protein-dominant fish dishes with small sugar-based glaze components. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Nutrient values adapted from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 175168 (Atlantic salmon, farmed, cooked, dry heat). Carbohydrate_g adjusted upward from 0 g (plain cooked salmon) to 4.5 g per 100 g to reflect the caramelisation glaze (mirin, sugar, soy sauce). Sodium_mg adjusted upward from ~59 mg (plain cooked salmon) to 380 mg per 100 g to reflect soy sauce in the glaze. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for farmed Atlantic salmon cooked by dry heat. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked salmon; fish does not contain meaningful vitamin C. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a pure fish and glaze dish with no plant fibre sources. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 175168 and are reported as numeric values. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record as all micronutrients are reported by the USDA source. Halal status is marked false as this dish may contain mirin (rice wine) in the glaze, which is not halal-certified by default; individual restaurant certification should be verified. GI is an analogous estimate at 35 (low category); GL per 150 g serving calculated as (35 × 6.75) / 100 = 2.4. The aliases_by_language chinese entry contains the Japanese-script name as a cross-reference; this is noted as an error in categorisation — the Japanese-script entry belongs under japanese only and has been corrected in aliases_by_language.
